content.text: refers to configuring the Playwright MCP (Model Context Protocol) server so Claude can programmatically control browsers.
are isolated, incognito-like environments within a single browser instance that provide test isolation.
with separate local storage, session storage, and cookies
Prevents cascading failures—if one test fails, it doesn't affect others
Enables reproducible debugging since you can run a single test independently
Fast and cheap to create, even running multiple contexts in a single browser
Playwright MCP Enables AI Browser Automation
Allows Claude to interact with web pages using structured accessibility snapshots (not pixels)
Works with Claude Code, Claude Desktop, VS Code, Cursor without vision models
Then restart Claude Code and ask:
"Navigate to https://example.com and take a screenshot"
Playwright MCP supports three profile configurations:
Working with your current browser tabs
Each context can emulate mobile devices, permissions, locale, and color schemes.
A useful mental model is three layers:[]
: interprets goals, plans steps, and emits structured commands (“open URL”, “click selector”, “extract data”).
: a Node/TS process that receives those commands and uses the Playwright API to operate pages and contexts.
: Chromium/WebKit/Firefox, local or remote (e.g., Browserless), hosting tabs/pages for each task.[]
Claude does not talk directly to the browser; it talks to your Playwright layer via tools or an MCP server, and your Playwright layer owns browser lifecycle, contexts, and low-level error handling.[]
For Claude Desktop / Claude Code, the most common integration is via a Playwright MCP server or similar agent tool:[]
or via the MCP server’s instructions).[]
Install a Playwright MCP or toolbox package, which exposes a small set of tool endpoints (open page, click, fill, query DOM, screenshot).[]
Register the MCP server in Claude’s config (e.g.,
server name to a command like
Once configured, Claude can discover the Playwright tools and invoke them via natural language (“Use Playwright to open
: Claude calls a Playwright MCP server, which hides browser details and exposes a focused tool surface (navigate, query DOM, etc.).
: A custom Node/TS service exposes HTTP or a bespoke protocol; Claude uses advanced tool use or programmatic tool calling to emit Playwright commands, and your service executes them.[]
For “Playwright + Claude in production,” the custom orchestrator pattern is common because it makes it easier to manage contexts, persistence, and remote browsers.[]
, and each context hosts one or more
: separate cookies, local storage, and cache per context, so different tasks/users do not share state.
: starting from a known context (or profile) ensures repeatable automation.
: you can map each customer, job, or environment to its own context.[]

In a Claude setup, a “browser context” usually maps 1:1 (or near) with a logical job or “world” that Claude is controlling: for example, “QA run for app X in staging” or “data extraction job for domain Y.”[]
One context per agent session or execution
When the harness starts a Claude job, it asks the Playwright layer to create a new context, and passes the resulting
back to Claude in the tool call or system prompt.[]
All subsequent Playwright tool calls for that job include this
Once Claude declares the task done (or the harness times out), the orchestrator closes the context and cleans up pages, preventing leaks.[]
This gives strong isolation with minimal complexity.
Keep a Playwright context tied to a user profile or storage directory (e.g.,
Use this context for tasks that require staying logged-in (internal dashboards, SaaS admin panels, etc.).[]
Fresh context with no cookies or storage, ideal for deterministic QA and scraping.[]
Always created and destroyed around a single job.

In Claude’s world, the harness decides which kind of context to ask for based on the workflow: ephemeral for tests and scraping, persistent for “operate this logged-in account with Claude watching.”[]
Mapping Claude sessions to Playwright contexts
how does Claude know which context it is operating in?
The harness creates or reuses a Playwright context and stores a mapping:
When Claude calls a Playwright tool (via MCP or PTC), your tool implementation injects the correct
before forwarding to your Playwright controller.[]
Claude itself usually only sees high-level descriptors (“current browser session”), not raw Playwright object IDs.
This keeps the Playwright details out of prompt space and in your code, while still letting Claude reason about “the current browser window” conceptually.[]
Within a context, you manage one or more Pages:[]
(simple case): Claude opens a single tab and does all work there: navigate, click, extract, etc.
: For more advanced tasks, the orchestrator might open additional pages (e.g., login provider window, secondary docs). Each page gets an ID or handle that the tool surface exposes.
page, with explicit tools to switch current page or open a new one.[]
Keep page switching explicit to Claude (“open a new tab for site B”) to avoid confusion between contexts and pages.
Using remote browsers and scaling contexts
The Playwright controller does not have to run the browser locally:[]
Remote providers like Browserless or custom Playwright services host browser instances; your Node service connects via WebSocket or similar.[]
This lets you scale browsers independently of Claude and the orchestrator, and run many contexts in parallel.
: Claude Opus/Sonnet, receiving the prompt and emitting commands.
: Playwright service that holds many contexts and pages across remote browsers.
: a fleet of headless browsers in a Kubernetes cluster or managed browser service.[]
Contexts become your main concurrency unit: each context can be scheduled, monitored, and torn down independently.
Robust context management for Claude + Playwright includes:[]
: Cap the maximum number of concurrent contexts per worker or per host to avoid exhausting memory.
: Give each context a maximum lifetime; close contexts that exceed this, and notify Claude (or the harness) of failure.
: On Playwright errors (navigation timeout, selector not found), return structured error results to Claude so it can retry, adjust strategy, or bail out.[]

For sensitive or high-risk environments, you can add policy checks at the context level (e.g., allowed domains per context, read-only operations) so Claude can’t use Playwright to wander off into unexpected sites.[]
mapping stored in memory or a store.
const page = await context.newPage(); await page.goto(url);

Claude uses advanced tool use or MCP tools to call these APIs.
The harness ensures each Claude job is tied to a specific
This pattern separates per-job browser state and ensures that a misbehaving workflow cannot pollute others.
: Reusing a single context for many tasks leads to unpredictable behavior, leaked logins, and cross-tenant contamination.
Letting Claude manage raw Playwright objects
: Forcing Claude to juggle context/page IDs directly instead of hiding them behind tools complicates prompts and increases errors.
Overloading context with full DOM dumps
: Some early MCP setups dump entire DOM trees into Claude’s context after every step, quickly hitting token limits; better to send targeted data or summaries.[][]
: Leaving contexts open indefinitely leaks memory and browser slots, eventually stalling the system.
Avoiding these mistakes makes browser automation more stable and cheaper.
Best practices for Playwright setup and contexts
: one per agent session or job, with explicit creation and teardown.
Decide early on where you need
(clean QA runs), and encode that in your orchestrator’s API.
that Claude uses (e.g., navigate, click, query, screenshot), and let your code handle context mapping and error handling.
when scaling beyond a handful of concurrent jobs, and monitor per-context resource usage and failure rates.[]
Log context lifecycle events (create, page open, actions, close) externally for observability, not by dumping everything into Claude’s token context.
, using Claude primarily for high-level planning and interpretation, not for driving every single low-level detail.
Managed this way, Playwright becomes a robust, scalable browser backend that Claude can reliably drive for testing, scraping, RPA-style workflows, and more.
: Each test has its own context with separate storage/cookies, preventing state leakage
: Add one config entry to Claude, restart, and you're ready to automate browsers
: Create separate contexts for admin/user scenarios like chat applications
: Claude interacts via structured page elements (roles, text, refs) rather than vision
: Playwright works with Chrome, Firefox, and WebKit out of the box
